summary: Mexico and the US wrapped a third round of US-Mexico-Canada Agreement review talks, with President Sheinbaum citing progress alongside US trade representative Jamieson Greer, but left tariffs and rules of origin for a fourth round in Washington in September. That pressure grew after Washington imposed a 10% tariff on Mexican exports outside the agreement over forced labor concerns. Separately, Mexico's electricity utility Mexico's state power company and Cubico signed a $1 billion renewable energy joint venture, while US and Mexican treasuries sanctioned over 50 people and companies tied to the Jalisco New Generation Cartel's financial network.

lead.h1: Mexico and US set fourth round of talks to review the US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement for September
lead.context: The two governments concluded a third round of trade treaty negotiations with President Sheinbaum citing progress, but the toughest issues on tariffs and rules of origin were left for a fourth round to be held in Washington in September.
lead.background: The review stems from the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement's first joint review, at which Washington declined a 16-year extension, leaving the pact subject to annual reviews while unresolved issues persist.
lead.drivers: Work aims to strengthen North American economic security and competitiveness through deeper supply-chain integration, as Washington pushes to cut reliance on Asian suppliers.
lead.implications: The talks proceeded even as the US formalized a 10% tariff on Mexican goods tied to forced labor.
lead.next: A fourth round covering tariffs and rules of origin is set for Washington in September.

coverage.title: Mexico and US set fourth round of USMCA review talks for September
coverage.summary: The two governments concluded a third round of trade treaty negotiations with President Sheinbaum citing progress, but the toughest issues on tariffs and rules of origin were left for a fourth round to be held in Washington in September.

items.headline: Sheinbaum says Mexico and US advance in talks to review the US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement
items.context: The meeting with US trade representative Jamieson Greer is part of the trade pact's first annual review, coming after Trump declined to extend the agreement for another 16 years.
items.background: The meeting was part of the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement's newly mandated annual review process, triggered after the US declined to extend the pact for another 16 years at the treaty's first joint review.
items.drivers: The talks brought together Mexico's foreign and economy secretaries with US trade office Jamieson Greer and the US ambassador at the National Palace for just over an hour.

coverage.title: Sheinbaum says Mexico and US advance in USMCA review talks
coverage.summary: The meeting with US trade representative Jamieson Greer is part of the trade pact's first annual review, coming after Trump declined to extend the agreement for another 16 years.

items.headline: United States imposes 10% tariff on Mexican exports outside US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement over forced labor concerns
items.context: The tariff applies to goods that fall outside the free-trade agreement and use inputs linked to forced labor, landing in the middle of the treaty's annual review and adding pressure on Mexico's trade negotiators.
items.background: The tariff stems from a US investigation into roughly 60 economies accused of failing to prevent forced labor in supply chains, part of an effort to rebuild tariff barriers weakened by the Supreme Court.
items.drivers: Countries deemed to have adopted forced-labor prohibitions face a 10% tariff, while dozens of others face 12.5%, applied to goods outside existing free-trade coverage.
items.implications: The measure adds a new cost on Mexican goods not covered by US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement just as the treaty's own review is underway.
items.next: The tariffs take effect Friday at 00:01 New York time.

coverage.title: United States imposes 10% tariff on Mexican exports outside USMCA over forced labor concerns
coverage.summary: The tariff applies to goods that fall outside the free-trade agreement and use inputs linked to forced labor, landing in the middle of the treaty's annual review and adding pressure on Mexico's trade negotiators.

items.headline: Mexico's state power company and Cubico sign $1 billion renewable energy joint venture
items.context: The deal, Mexico's first mixed public-private renewable contract of its kind, will deploy 578 megawatts of renewable capacity and 500 megawatt-hours of battery storage across Tamaulipas, Nuevo León, Campeche and the Yucatan peninsula.
items.background: Mexico's state power company is Mexico's state electricity utility, and the deal uses the March 2025 Secondary Energy Legislation that opened a structured path for private capital to partner with state-led power planning.
items.drivers: The joint venture targets nearshoring-driven power demand in Tamaulipas, Nuevo León, Campeche and the Yucatán Peninsula.
items.implications: The 25-year partnership signals a structured path for private capital investment aligned with state-led planning rather than fully private generation.

coverage.title: CFE and Cubico sign $1 billion renewable energy joint venture
coverage.summary: The deal, Mexico's first mixed public-private renewable contract of its kind, will deploy 578 megawatts of renewable capacity and 500 megawatt-hours of battery storage across Tamaulipas, Nuevo León, Campeche and the Yucatan peninsula.

items.headline: US and Mexican treasuries sanction Jalisco cartel's financial network
items.context: The action targets more than 50 people and companies tied to the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, including the stepson of its leader known as 'El Mencho,' marking one of the largest joint financial strikes against the group.
items.background: The Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG) is one of Mexico's largest criminal organizations, led by Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, known as 'El Mencho'.
items.drivers: Mexico's Financial Intelligence Unit filed a complaint with the federal attorney general's office alleging money laundering tied to the sanctioned network, while the US Treasury froze the assets of those listed.
items.implications: The sanctions cut off a network of individuals and companies accused of financing the cartel through money laundering.
items.next: Mexico's Attorney General's office will pursue prosecution of the sanctioned individuals following the Financial Intelligence Unit's complaint.

coverage.title: US and Mexican treasuries sanction Jalisco cartel's financial network
coverage.summary: The action targets more than 50 people and companies tied to the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, including the stepson of its leader known as 'El Mencho,' marking one of the largest joint financial strikes against the group.
